symbols: platter with breasts — the martyrdom of Saint Agatha whose breasts were cut off during torture
red cloak — martyrdom and the shedding of blood for faith
serene expression — the peace and courage of a martyr who endures through faith
elegant dress — Agatha's noble birth and status in Catania
arched panel format — devotional altarpiece context
description: A young woman stands in an arched niche against a very dark background. She wears a dark bodice with golden-orange puffed sleeves, a long pinkish-mauve skirt, and a vivid red cloak draped over one shoulder. She holds a plate or platter in her extended right hand bearing what appear to be two rounded forms. She wears a necklace and has her dark hair parted in the center. Her expression is calm and composed, gazing slightly downward toward the viewer.
theological_description: Saint Agatha of Sicily (died c. 251 AD) was a Christian virgin martyr who refused the advances of the Roman prefect Quintianus. As punishment, her breasts were severed — the attribute she holds on the platter. While not a biblical figure, her story of faithfulness under persecution resonates with the biblical call to endure suffering for righteousness. Jesus warned His followers 'they will deliver you up to tribulation and put you to death, and you will be hated by all nations for my name's sake' (Matt 24:9). The Book of Revelation praises those 'who had been slain for the word of God and for the witness they had borne' (Rev 6:9). Agatha's composure in Zurbaran's depiction reflects the Pauline conviction that 'to live is Christ and to die is gain' (Phil 1:21). Her story became a powerful witness in the early Church that bodily suffering could not overcome spiritual devotion.

scripture_refs.reason: They will deliver you to tribulation and death for my name's sake — martyrdom
Souls under the altar who had been slain for the word of God
To live is Christ and to die is gain — the martyr's conviction
